name: Epinephrine
indication: Cardiac arrest (all rhythms); symptomatic bradycardia infusion.
dose: 1 mg IV/IO push every 3–5 min during arrest. Infusion 2–10 mcg/min for bradycardia/hypotension.
caution: Standard vasopressor of ACLS — not a substitute for compressions; give right after compressions resume, not during the rhythm-check pause.
mnemonic: "1 milligram, every light change" — 3 to 5 minutes.

name: Amiodarone
indication: Refractory VF/pVT (after 3rd shock); stable wide-complex tachycardia.
dose: Arrest: 300 mg IV/IO bolus, then 150 mg for a second dose. Non-arrest infusion: 150 mg over 10 min.
caution: Causes hypotension when pushed fast; peripheral infusions running long can cause phlebitis — flag for a central line if continuing past the code.
mnemonic: "300 the first time, cut it in half the second" — 300 → 150.

name: Lidocaine
indication: Alternative to amiodarone for refractory VF/pVT.
dose: First dose 1–1.5 mg/kg IV/IO. Second dose 0.5–0.75 mg/kg.
caution: Weight-based, unlike amiodarone's flat dosing — don't mix the two drugs' math up under pressure.

name: Atropine
indication: Symptomatic bradycardia. NOT used in PEA/asystole.
dose: 1 mg IV/IO, repeat every 3–5 min, maximum total 3 mg.
caution: Unlikely to help high-grade block (Mobitz II, 3rd-degree with wide QRS) — don't delay pacing waiting on repeat doses.
mnemonic: "One at a time, up to three."

name: Adenosine
indication: Stable narrow-regular tachycardia (SVT), after vagal maneuvers fail.
dose: 6 mg rapid IV push immediately followed by a saline flush; if no conversion, 12 mg.
caution: Very short half-life — push fast, flush faster. Reduce dose for transplanted hearts; caution in reactive airway disease (bronchospasm risk). Warn the patient about a brief pause/flushing sensation.
mnemonic: "6, then 12 — push it fast, flush it faster."

name: Dopamine
indication: Symptomatic bradycardia unresponsive to atropine, as an alternative/adjunct to pacing.
caution: Titrate to the lowest effective rate — tachyarrhythmia and ectopy increase at higher doses.

name: Magnesium sulfate
dose: 1–2 g IV over 5–60 min; give as a faster push over a few minutes if pulseless.
caution: Watch for hypotension and loss of deep tendon reflexes with rapid or high-dose administration.

name: Calcium chloride / Sodium bicarbonate
indication: Hyperkalemia, calcium-channel-blocker or beta-blocker overdose, tricyclic overdose, known pre-existing severe acidosis.
dose: Not weight-standardized for routine arrest use — dosed to the specific indication.
caution: Not given routinely in cardiac arrest — reserve for a specifically identified "T" or "H" that calls for it.
mnemonic: "Not routine — only for the special H's and T's."

name: Aspirin
dose: 160–325 mg, non-enteric, chewed for fast absorption.
caution: Confirm no true aspirin allergy or active major bleed.
mnemonic: "First and fast" — the A in ANOM.

name: Nitroglycerin
dose: 0.4 mg sublingual, may repeat every 5 min up to 3 doses.
caution: Hold if SBP < 90, suspected right ventricular infarct, or a PDE5 inhibitor (sildenafil-class) was taken in the last 24–48 h — risk of profound hypotension.
mnemonic: "No nitro on a soft pressure or a little blue pill."

name: Oxygen
dose: Titrate to SpO₂ 92–98%; not given routinely to normoxic patients.
caution: Routine high-flow O₂ in a non-hypoxic ACS patient offers no benefit and may increase infarct size.

name: Naloxone
dose: 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/IN; may repeat. Give without interrupting ventilations/CPR.
caution: Doesn't harm a non-opioid arrest — when uncertain, give it and keep bagging.
mnemonic: "When in doubt, give it and keep breathing for them."
